message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    In people with COVID-19, hypoxia at the time of admission is known to be related to mortality. Monitoring of oxygen saturations (SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    ) is therefore an increasingly common part of community-based care, with the aim of improving the identification of adults who are deteriorating. We set out to investigate whether rigid SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    triggers, or absolute change in SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    , is more indicative of deterioration in COVID-19.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A prospective, uncontrolled, open-label study in a large UK general practice was conducted between May and November 2020. Participants recorded twice daily oximetry and symptom diary for 14 days after test-confirmed COVID-19. Primary outcomes were the proportion of people whose SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    dropped to ≤ 94% and ≤ 92%, the average maximum reduction in SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    , and admission to hospital. We also investigated the relationship between MRC Dyspnoea scale, modified Roth score, and SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    through correlation analyses.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    52 participants were recruited, following which 41 participants completed the study. The average age was 45.9 years with 63.4% identifying as female. The mean maximum reduction in SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    was 2.8%. The average time to maximum reduction in SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    was 6.4 days. Nine participants (22.0%) had a reduction in SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    to ≤94%. Three of these had a reduction in SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    to ≤92%, for which all three were admitted to hospital. Modified Roth score and SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    were weakly positively correlated (.31). MRC dyspnoea scale score and SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    were moderately negatively correlated (-.53).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A reduction in SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    to ≤92% was found to be highly predictive for admission to hospital. Modified Roth score or MRC dyspnoea scale scores should not be used as proxy measures for oximetry. This study contributes to the ongoing narrative around community-based oximetry and provides insight and recommendations for those currently engaging in or planning to roll out similar schemes.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Strengths and limitations of this study</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>This study is pragmatically designed to answer an important clinical question in primary care.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        This study focused on previously published values of SpO
                        <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                        for triggering escalation of care and therefore provides answers based on current clinical practice.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>11 of the 52 patients who were recruited into the study did not return their oximeter or oximetry diary at the end of the study period.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>We did not validate the accuracy or reliability of the oximetry / symptom diaries, as these were self-completed by the participants themselves.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Other than admission to hospital and mortality within the study period, no other clinical outcomes have been recorded.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Funding statement</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This research received no specific grant from any funding agency in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Competing interests</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Jane Wilcock has no competing interests to declare.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Ciaran Grafton-Clarke has no competing interests to declare.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Tessa Coulson has no competing interests to declare.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: What is the value of community oximetry monitoring in people with SARS-CoV-2? – A prospective, open-label clinical study

reference.unstructured: NHS England. Pulse oximetry to detect early deterioration of patients with Covid-19 in primary and community care settings. 2020. Available at: https://www.england.nhs.uk/coronavirus/wp-content/uploads/sites/52/2020/06/C0445-remote-monitoring-in-primary-care-revised.pdf
Greenhalgh T KK , Van Der Westhuizen HM . Are there any evidence-based ways of assessing dyspnoea (breathlessness) by telephone or video? Oxford Covid-19 Evidence Service Rapid Review 2020
IBM Corporate. IBM SPSS Statistics for Windows, Version 27.0. 2020
